headline: Chips routed as money hides in the boring stuff
body_html:
Markets turned defensive on Tuesday as a sharp pullback in semiconductors dragged the broad tape lower, even while the classic safe-harbor corners of the market held their ground. Across the broad universe, decliners outnumbered advancers by roughly three to two — and almost all of the damage was concentrated in one place.

Chips took it on the chin. The semiconductor group fell about 8.5% on average, led lower by a brutal double-digit slide in Micron as the memory trade unwound and investors questioned how much of this year's AI-hardware enthusiasm was already in the price. It was the kind of session that punishes crowding, and the most crowded trade on the board paid for it.

The rotation had a familiar shape. Consumer Staples — the place money goes when conviction fades — topped the board, up roughly 1.8%, with Healthcare close behind. CDW bucked the gloom to lead all names higher. Capital moved toward balance sheets and dividends and away from the year's hottest momentum names, in a tape that rewarded patience over pace.

briefs.headline: Memory names lead the chip selloff
briefs.body: Micron paced a double-digit retreat across semiconductors as the memory trade unwound, dragging the group down roughly 8.5% on average. After a year in which chips carried much of the market's gains, the most crowded corner of the tape was the first to be sold.

briefs.headline: Defensives catch the bid
briefs.body: Consumer Staples and Healthcare finished higher as money rotated out of high-beta growth and into balance sheets and dividends. On a red day for the broad market, the defensive sectors did exactly what they are supposed to do — hold the line.

briefs.headline: Energy stays the macro wildcard
briefs.body: Crude remained in focus amid Middle East supply concerns, with refiners reportedly reworking sourcing. Oil is the swing factor for the inflation path — and, by extension, for how soon the Fed feels free to ease — so the energy tape carries weight well beyond its own sector.

briefs.headline: Breadth turns decidedly negative
briefs.body: Decliners outpaced advancers by about three to two across the universe, a wider spread than the headline index moves suggested. When the average stock falls harder than the benchmark, it usually points to a narrow, top-heavy market working off excess.

briefs.headline: CDW leads a thin list of winners
briefs.body: CDW topped the entire board on a day when green was scarce, a reminder that even broad selloffs leave pockets of strength. Names with steady, less cyclical demand profiles drew buyers looking for somewhere to hide.

briefs.headline: Growth and software feel the rotation
briefs.body: The pressure that hit chips spilled into the broader growth complex, with richly valued software names giving ground as investors reassessed how much future growth they were willing to pay up for. The move was less about earnings and more about appetite for risk.
